quote: If you'd told me I'd be a memory champion, I'd have laughed at you.
tension: Six-time champion. He says he started with a memory no better than yours.
situation: Asked when he realized he was born with a superhuman memory, Nelson's answer was blunt: he *wasn't*. Before he found memory techniques, he'd have called his own memory *average* and laughed at the idea of a title. What started him was watching his grandmother struggle with Alzheimer's.
principle: He *built* the memory that won six championships. He trained an average memory into a record-breaking one, and his message is that the raw capacity was never rare: *it's within all of us*.
brain: Champions don't run on different hardware. Brain scans of memory athletes show *ordinary* structure. Their edge is a *learned technique* that routes new facts through the brain's spatial and visual systems, the same ones you use to find your way home. The machinery was always there.
reframe: If a man who called his own memory average can build a record-breaking one, the limit you assume about yourself is probably imaginary. What looks like a gift is usually a *method, practiced*. Your memory can be *trained* the same way, starting now.
ctaPunch: And that's only the *first five minutes* of the conversation.
